title: Side Hustle Tax Calculator (USA) 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Calculate self-employment tax, federal income tax, and net income from your side hustle. Estimates SE tax (15.3%), QBI deduction, and quarterly payments for…
intro: Running a side hustle means the IRS treats you as self-employed — even for part-time gigs. On top of your regular federal income tax, you owe **self-employment (SE) tax of 15.3%** on net profit (Social Security 12.4% up to $176,100 + Medicare 2.9% unlimited). This calculator shows your real tax bill, the deductions you qualify for, and how much you actually keep after taxes.
keyTakeaway: A side hustle earning $30,000 net in the 22% bracket typically owes ~$4,239 SE tax + ~$4,559 federal income tax = ~$8,798 total, keeping roughly $21,202 — about 70.7 cents on the dollar.
useCases: Freelancer estimating quarterly estimated tax payments to avoid IRS penalties
Gig worker (Uber, DoorDash, Etsy) calculating true after-tax hourly rate
Side business owner deciding whether to incorporate as an S-Corp to reduce SE tax
Employee with a side hustle estimating additional taxes before year-end
1099 contractor comparing W-2 job offer vs. staying self-employed
Blogger or content creator tracking deductible business expenses vs. gross revenue

options.label: Yes — I qualify (income below phase-out limits)
No — I don't qualify or prefer to skip

outputs.label: Federal Income Tax on Side Income
State Income Tax on Side Income

explanation:
## How the Calculator Works

Side hustle income is reported on **Schedule C** (Profit or Loss from Business). Net profit equals gross income minus deductible business expenses. All taxes apply to this net figure.

## Formula

```
Net Profit = Gross Income − Business Expenses

SE Tax Base  = Net Profit × 0.9235          // IRS multiplier (92.35%)
SE Tax       = SE Tax Base × 0.153          // 12.4% SS + 2.9% Medicare

Half SE Tax Deduction = SE Tax / 2          // Above-the-line deduction (Schedule 1)

QBI Deduction (if eligible) = (Net Profit − Half SE Deduction) × 0.20

Taxable Side Income = Net Profit
                    − Half SE Tax Deduction
                    − QBI Deduction

Federal Income Tax = Taxable Side Income × Marginal Bracket
State Income Tax   = Net Profit × State Rate

Total Tax = SE Tax + Federal Income Tax + State Income Tax
Net Keep  = Net Profit − Total Tax

Quarterly Payment = Total Tax / 4
```

## Worked Example

Assume: $30,000 gross, $5,000 expenses, 22% bracket, 5% state, QBI applied.

| Step | Amount |
|---|---|
| Net Profit | $25,000 |
| SE Tax Base (×0.9235) | $23,087.50 |
| SE Tax (×0.153) | $3,532.39 |
| Half SE Deduction | $1,766.19 |
| QBI Base ($25,000 − $1,766.19) | $23,233.81 |
| QBI Deduction (×0.20) | $4,646.76 |
| Taxable Side Income | $18,587.05 |
| Federal Income Tax (×0.22) | $4,089.15 |
| State Tax ($25,000 × 0.05) | $1,250.00 |
| **Total Tax** | **$8,871.54** |
| **Net Keep** | **$16,128.46** |
| Quarterly Payment | $2,217.89 |

## 2026 Key Thresholds

- **Social Security wage base**: $176,100 (income above this pays only the 2.9% Medicare portion)
- **Additional Medicare Tax**: 0.9% on self-employment income exceeding $200,000 (single) / $250,000 (MFJ) — not included in this basic calculator
- **QBI phase-out**: begins at $197,300 (single) / $394,600 (MFJ) for specified service trades

## Limitations & When NOT to Rely Solely on This Tool

- This calculator uses your **marginal bracket** for the side income estimate. If the side income pushes you into a higher bracket mid-range, results will be slightly overstated.
- The **QBI deduction** has income-based phase-outs and excludes certain service businesses (law, finance, consulting above thresholds). Verify eligibility with a tax professional.
- **S-Corp election** can reduce SE tax for high earners (typically worthwhile above ~$50,000 net profit) — this calculator does not model that strategy.
- State calculations use a flat rate; many states use graduated brackets or allow different deductions.
- Does not account for **self-employed health insurance deduction**, **SEP-IRA contributions**, or **home office deduction** — all of which can further reduce taxable income.

faq.q: Why is the SE tax base multiplied by 92.35% instead of the full net profit?
faq.a: The IRS allows you to reduce net profit by 7.65% before calculating SE tax. This mirrors how W-2 employees only pay their half of FICA (7.65%) — employers pay the other half. Self-employed individuals get an equivalent adjustment. The factor is 1 − 0.0765 = 0.9235. See IRS Schedule SE instructions.

faq.q: What counts as a deductible business expense for a side hustle?
faq.a: Ordinary and necessary expenses directly tied to your business. Common examples: software subscriptions, home office (dedicated space), equipment, vehicle mileage (67 cents/mile in 2024; check 2026 IRS rate), professional services, advertising, and supplies. Personal expenses are never deductible. Keep receipts and bank records.

faq.q: Do I owe SE tax if my side hustle nets less than $400?
faq.a: No. The IRS does not require you to file Schedule SE or pay SE tax if your net self-employment income is under $400 for the year. However, you still must report the income on Schedule C.

faq.q: What is the QBI deduction and do all self-employed people qualify?
faq.a: The 20% Qualified Business Income (QBI) deduction under IRC §199A lets eligible self-employed individuals deduct 20% of qualified business income from taxable income. It phases out for Specified Service Trades or Businesses (SSTBs) — lawyers, doctors, financial advisers, consultants — once income exceeds $197,300 (single, 2026). Non-SSTB businesses have different W-2/property limits at higher incomes.

faq.q: When are quarterly estimated tax payments due in 2026?
faq.a: The four 2026 due dates are: April 15, June 16, September 15, and January 15, 2027. You owe estimated payments if you expect to owe at least $1,000 in federal taxes after withholding. Use IRS Form 1040-ES to calculate and submit. Missing payments triggers an underpayment penalty (currently ~8% annualized).

faq.q: I also have a W-2 job. How does that affect my SE tax?
faq.a: SE tax is calculated only on your self-employment net profit, regardless of W-2 wages. However, your W-2 wages count toward the $176,100 Social Security wage base. If your W-2 wages already reach $176,100, the 12.4% SS portion of SE tax on your side income is zero (only the 2.9% Medicare portion applies). The calculator does not model W-2 wages; consult IRS Schedule SE Part I for this calculation.

faq.q: Can I reduce SE tax by forming an S-Corporation?
faq.a: Yes. An S-Corp pays you a 'reasonable salary' (subject to payroll taxes) and the remaining profit as a distribution (not subject to SE tax). At ~$50,000+ net profit, the savings can exceed S-Corp costs (~$1,500–$2,500/year in filing fees and accounting). This is a legitimate and common strategy, but requires payroll setup and additional filings. Consult a CPA before electing S-Corp status.

faq.q: What is the Additional Medicare Tax and when does it apply?
faq.a: The 0.9% Additional Medicare Tax applies to self-employment income exceeding $200,000 (single filers) or $250,000 (married filing jointly) combined across all income. This calculator excludes it. If your total income approaches those thresholds, add 0.9% on the amount over the limit manually.

faq.q: Does this calculator apply to gig workers on 1099-NEC or 1099-K forms?
faq.a: Yes. Income reported on a 1099-NEC (nonemployee compensation) or 1099-K (payment card/third-party network, threshold $5,000 for 2024–2025, $600 from 2026) is treated as self-employment income. The same SE tax, deduction, and quarterly payment rules apply. Always reconcile 1099 totals with your own records — platforms may report gross before their fees.

faq.q: What state tax rate should I enter if my state has no income tax?
faq.a: Enter 0. States with no personal income tax include Alaska, Florida, Nevada, South Dakota, Tennessee, Texas, Washington, and Wyoming. New Hampshire taxes only interest and dividends (not wages/self-employment income as of 2025). If your state uses graduated brackets, enter your effective marginal rate for the most accurate estimate.

dataUpdate.notes: Update Social Security wage base ($176,100 for 2026), QBI phase-out thresholds ($197,300/$394,600), federal tax brackets, and Additional Medicare Tax thresholds each January when IRS publishes annual inflation adjustments.
